emergency-responders/air/civil-air-patro.content:
Civil Air Patrol
The Civil Air Patrol (CAP) is the U.S. Air Force auxiliary — a volunteer organization that conducts approximately 85% of inland search and rescue missions in the U.S. CAP operates the largest fleet of single-engine aircraft for SAR in the world.
Operational Relevance
CAP flies search patterns with Cessna 172 and 182 aircraft — slower and lower than Coast Guard fixed-wing. This is directly relevant to Beacon’s visibility from light aircraft at pattern altitude. CAP crews use visual spotting as their primary search technique — an active strobe is the most detectable signal.
Engagement Status
No current engagement. CAP Florida Wing is a potential coalition partner. The St. Pete Hub is within CAP’s operational area.
Related

SAR Coalition — CAP as potential partner
Visibility range — light aircraft search altitude

emergency-responders/communications/apco.content:
APCO International
The Association of Public-Safety Communications Officials (APCO) sets standards for public safety communications — 911 dispatch, emergency alerting, and interoperability. APCO standards inform how distress signals are received and processed at the dispatch level.
Related

REACT International — volunteer counterpart

emergency-responders/communications/reac.content:
REACT International
REACT (Radio Emergency Associated Communication Teams) is a volunteer CB radio emergency monitoring organization. REACT members monitor CB Channel 9 (emergency channel) and provide communication support during disasters.
Relevance to Beacon is indirect — REACT represents the broader emergency communications community that might integrate visual signaling into their response toolkit.
Related

APCO International — public safety communications counterpart

emergency-responders/international/cospa.content:
COSPAS-SARSAT
The International Satellite System for Search and Rescue. A satellite network that detects and locates 406 MHz emergency beacons (EPIRBs, ELTs, PLBs). COSPAS-SARSAT is the global standard for satellite-based distress alerting.
How It Works
A 406 MHz beacon transmits a distress signal with GPS coordinates. MEOSAR satellites detect the alert within minutes. The position is relayed to a rescue coordination center, which dispatches SAR assets to the last known position.
What it gives you: a point-in-time GPS fix — the location where the beacon was when it transmitted. Accurate to within 1-3 nautical miles.
What it doesn’t give you: a track. The fix is a snapshot, not a moving update. In water, the victim drifts away from that fix within minutes. On land, a lost person keeps walking. SAR arrives at the last known position and must then search visually.
Beacon’s Relationship
A Coast Guard responder carries four tools on duty: signal mirror, strobe light, whistle, EPIRB. They’re complementary — each closes a different part of the search. The EPIRB brings rescuers near via satellite. The strobe marks position. The mirror catches sun. The whistle carries sound at close range.
Beacon is a mirror and SOS strobe in one — the civilian-side version of that kit. What you’d carry when you’re off duty, on the water, and you’re the one who needs to be found.
The 406 MHz alert gets SAR nearby. Beacon closes the last mile from the other side of the rescue.
No 406 MHz integration planned. Beacon operates in the visual/IR domain. Integrating 406 MHz would add cost, regulatory complexity, and false-alert risk. The satellite system and the visual strobe serve different phases of the same rescue.
Related

Aircraft ditching — COSPAS-SARSAT response to ELT activation
Man overboard at night — drift problem in water
IMO standards — maritime beacon requirements

emergency-responders/international/cospa.filePath: Emergency Responders/International/COSPAS-SARSAT/COSPAS-SARSAT S.007 Handbook of Beacon Regulations.md
emergency-responders/international/cospa.content:
COSPAS-SARSAT Handbook of Beacon Regulations (S.007)
Source: Handbook of Beacon Regulations, C/S S.007, Issue 2, Revision 4, October 2022. International Cospas-Sarsat Programme.
Scope
This document summarizes regulations issued by Cospas-Sarsat Participants regarding the carriage of 406 MHz beacons. It covers EPIRBs (Emergency Position-Indicating Radio Beacons), ELTs (Emergency Locator Transmitters), and PLBs (Personal Locator Beacons).
Three Beacon Types
The Cospas-Sarsat system recognizes three classes of 406 MHz distress beacon:

EPIRB — Maritime. Linked to a vessel via MMSI or radio call sign. Mandated by SOLAS for certain vessels.
ELT — Aviation. Linked to an aircraft via 24-bit address or registration marking. Mandated by ICAO.
PLB — Personal. “Intended for use by an individual person (i.e., not necessarily linked to a ship or an aircraft like EPIRBs and ELTs). They can be used in any environment (e.g., on land, at sea and in aircraft).”

Beacon relevance: Beacon is closest to the PLB category — a personal signaling device for an individual, usable in any environment. However, Beacon operates in the visual/IR domain rather than 406 MHz radio. Beacon addresses the gap Cospas-Sarsat acknowledges: a 406 MHz alert brings rescuers to a GPS fix, but the victim drifts away from that fix. Beacon marks the victim’s actual position with light.
Critical Limitation

No beacon transmits properly under water.

Beacon relevance: This single sentence from the international beacon authority validates Beacon’s fundamental design premise. 406 MHz signals cannot transmit through water. A person in the water — the most common paddlesport emergency — cannot rely on satellite beacons for continuous position marking. Beacon’s visual strobe operates above water (on the PFD shoulder), providing continuous position marking where RF beacons fail.
False Activation Consequences

Activating a 406 MHz beacon for even a very short time will generate a Cospas-Sarsat distress alert message that will be relayed to SAR services for their immediate action. Activating a beacon for reasons other than to indicate a distress situation or without the prior authorization from a Cospas-Sarsat MCC is considered an offence in many countries/territories of the world, and could result in prosecution.

Beacon relevance: 406 MHz beacons carry a heavy deterrent against false activation — criminal prosecution. This creates a psychological barrier: a person who is unsure whether their situation qualifies as “distress” may delay activation. Beacon has no such barrier. A strobe is a local visual signal — it does not trigger a satellite alert, does not involve law enforcement, and can be activated at any level of concern without consequences. This lowers the threshold for signaling and increases the likelihood of early activation.
Self-Test Capability

406 MHz beacons are designed with a self-test capability for evaluating key performance characteristics. Initiating the beacon self-test function will not generate a distress alert in the Cospas-Sarsat System.

Beacon relevance: Self-test without false alert is a design requirement proven by 406 MHz beacons. Beacon’s firmware should include a self-test mode (battery check, LED test) that does not produce an SOS pattern — matching the industry standard established by Cospas-Sarsat.
Scale of the System

There are more than two million Cospas-Sarsat 406 MHz distress beacons in operation.

Beacon relevance: The 406 MHz ecosystem is massive — over 2 million beacons worldwide. Beacon does not compete with this system; it complements it. The visual strobe addresses the gap between satellite fix and visual acquisition that 2 million beacons cannot close.
Return Link Service (RLS)

The Return Link Service (RLS) provides notification to a 406 MHz beacon that an alert transmitted by the beacon has been detected. This service is intended to provide acknowledgement of the reception of the alert message to persons in distress.

Beacon relevance: RLS provides psychological reassurance — “your alert was received.” Beacon cannot provide this for satellite alerts (no 406 MHz integration), but its visual strobe provides a different form of reassurance: the user can see the light flashing and know the signal is active. For IR strobe, the reassurance comes from knowing SAR aircraft use IR detection.
PLB Registration Requirements

PLBs are intended for use by an individual person. Emergency contacts listed in the registration record of the PLB should speak the official language(s) associated with the country code of the beacon.

Beacon relevance: 406 MHz PLBs require registration — a bureaucratic step before the device works. Beacon requires no registration. Buy it, attach it, activate it. This is a meaningful market differentiation for casual outdoor users who would not register a PLB.
Beacon Coding Complexity
The S.007 details multiple coding protocols (User Protocols and Location Protocols) across EPIRBs, ELTs, and PLBs — each with different country-specific requirements. This complexity is inherent to the 406 MHz system and is a barrier to adoption for recreational users.
Beacon relevance: Every coding protocol, every country-specific regulation, every registration requirement is friction that Beacon eliminates. Beacon has no coding, no protocols, no registration. It simply produces light in an internationally recognized distress pattern (SOS).

emergency-responders/international/icao-.content:
ICAO SAR Provisions
The International Civil Aviation Organization establishes global standards for aeronautical search and rescue. ICAO Annex 12 defines the SAR framework for civil aviation — including alerting, search, and rescue phases.
Beacon’s IR strobe is directly relevant to ICAO SAR provisions for night aerial search. The IR signal through NVG is the most effective visual detection method at search altitude.
Related

COSPAS-SARSAT — ICAO participates in the COSPAS-SARSAT program
IMO standards — maritime counterpart
Aircraft ditching — ICAO SAR response scenario

emergency-responders/international/imo-s.content:
IMO Standards
The International Maritime Organization sets global standards for maritime safety. IMO regulations relevant to Beacon include the International Code of Signals (recognizes SOS) and SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) provisions for visual distress signals.
Beacon is not SOLAS-compliant (SOLAS requires pyrotechnic flares for certain applications). Beacon is designed as a supplemental electronic signal — not a replacement for SOLAS-required equipment on regulated vessels.
Related

USCG SAR Doctrine — U.S. implementation of IMO standards
COSPAS-SARSAT — satellite system
ICAO SAR provisions — aviation counterpart

emergency-responders/land/bsa.content:
Boy Scouts of America (Scouting America)
Scouting America (formerly Boy Scouts of America) is the largest youth outdoor education organization in the United States. Through its merit badge program, it formally trains Scouts in search and rescue, emergency preparedness, first aid, lifesaving, and wilderness survival — skills that directly intersect with SAR operations.
BSA is not a SAR organization itself, but its training materials reflect real SAR doctrine. The BSA Search and Rescue merit badge pamphlet teaches the Incident Command System, search tactics, probability of detection, and gear requirements that professional SAR teams use. Many SAR volunteers are former Scouts, and BSA high-adventure bases (Philmont, Northern Tier, Sea Base) maintain their own SAR teams.
Coalition Position
BSA’s value to the SAR guidance coalition is doctrinal amplification. BSA’s merit badge pamphlets reach hundreds of thousands of Scouts annually. When BSA teaches that a whistle and signal mirror are mandatory SAR gear, that becomes the baseline expectation for a generation of future SAR volunteers and outdoor enthusiasts.
Published Materials
Source Materials Inventoried

Key Doctrinal Signals
“Whistle to signal for help” — BSA’s Family Disaster Kit explicitly designates the whistle as a signaling tool. Both the SAR ready pack and disaster kit mandate whistle + light sources.
“Three of anything — visual or audible — means distress” — BSA teaches the international SOS standard across multiple pamphlets. Flashing SOS in Morse code is taught for “night or day.”
“Signaling with a mirror can save your life” — Mirror signaling technique formally taught in Emergency Preparedness. V-finger sighting method.
“85 percent of canoeing victims and 48 percent of kayaking victims were not wearing life jackets” — Direct paddlesport drowning data cited in Lifesaving pamphlet.
“Active drowning victims cannot reach for aids” — Validates that signaling must be passive (attached to victim, not requiring reaching/grasping).
Coalition Notes

BSA high-adventure bases maintain active SAR teams (PhilSAR at Philmont is the most prominent)
BSA’s Ready Pack gear list aligns with professional SAR team requirements
BSA teaches ICS — the same command system used by USCG, NIMS, and all federal SAR
“Cotton kills” doctrine (Search and Rescue pamphlet) is standard wilderness medicine

emergency-responders/land/bsa/bsa-emerge.content:
BSA Emergency Preparedness
Source: Emergency Preparedness, Scouting America Merit Badge Series, 2026 printing, 96 pages. ISBN 978-0-8395-3368-9.
Five Aspects of Emergency Preparedness

This is what emergency preparedness is all about: preparing for, responding to, recovering from, mitigating, and preventing emergency situations. Whether an emergency involves your family or your entire community, on highways or waterways, in your home or outdoors, you can bring your Scouting skills and knowledge to the situation and help.

Beacon relevance: The five-aspect framework places Beacon squarely in “Respond” — the moment when an emergency is in progress and signaling matters. Beacon doesn’t prevent or mitigate; it responds with light. The pamphlet’s framing validates that response tools have a defined place in emergency preparation.
Family Disaster Kit — Mandatory Signaling
The BSA-specified Family Disaster Kit includes, among its items:

■ Whistle to signal for help
■ Flashlight or lantern or chemical light sticks
■ Extra batteries
■ Battery- or crank-powered radio
■ Matches in waterproof container and fire starting kit

Beacon relevance: The whistle is explicitly designated “to signal for help” — its purpose in the kit is signaling, not recreation. Light sources are mandatory for visibility and rescue signaling. This is the same dual signaling system Beacon provides: audio (whistle integrated) and visual (strobe). The Family Disaster Kit is the civilian/home analogue to the SAR ready pack described in the Search and Rescue pamphlet — both mandate whistle and light sources for signaling.
Personal Emergency Service Bag

Be prepared for a mobilization call with a personal emergency service bag. You will be ready for many emergencies if you use the following checklist.

Includes: poncho, change of clothes, sleeping bag, maps, pocket knife, flashlight, battery-powered radio, extra batteries, personal first-aid kit, matches, emergency ration, compass, pencil/notebook, work gloves, dust masks, boots, long-sleeved shirt.
Beacon relevance: The personal emergency service bag is what a Scout would grab when mobilized for disaster response. The flashlight is the only active signaling tool in the bag — everything else is sustainment. Beacon adds an active visual signal (strobe) and IR detection capability to this loadout without adding weight or complexity.
Emergency in the Outdoors — Lost or Marooned

If you become lost or marooned with a group, be a leader. Stay calm and help others stay calm. Usually, it is best to stay put. People searching can find you easier than you can find them.


If there is any possibility that you will have to stay out overnight, find a good campsite before dusk. Do not travel at night, except in the desert with good visibility.

Beacon relevance: The “stay put” doctrine is central to Beacon’s use case. A lost subject who stays put is a stationary target — the ideal scenario for a strobe. Searchers move toward the light; the subject doesn’t move away. The warning against traveling at night aligns with Beacon’s night operation: a stationary strobe at night is the highest-contrast signal possible.
Plane Signals — The Universal Language

Three fires arranged in a triangle convey a universal SOS. Three of anything — visual or audible — means “distress.”


Flashing SOS using Morse code — three short flashes, three long flashes (twice as long as the short ones), three short flashes — is another way to attract help, night or day. The distress answer is two of anything.


Signaling with a mirror can save your life, but you must know how to aim it effectively toward your rescuer.

Beacon relevance: This is the BSA’s formal teaching on visual distress signaling — and it maps directly to Beacon’s SOS strobe pattern. “Three of anything — visual or audible — means distress” is the international standard Beacon implements. The SOS Morse code flash pattern “night or day” is exactly what Beacon produces. The pamphlet validates both the pattern (SOS) and the medium (light flash) as recognized distress signals. The mirror signaling technique taught here is the same principle Beacon’s overt signal mirror uses.
Ground-to-Air Symbols

In the daytime, a ground-level “sign language” of symbols can attract an aircraft. Because geometric figures are not found in nature, symbols such as squares and triangles will attract attention. Make the symbols big — 10 feet wide or wider — in an open area where they can be seen.


When in doubt, use the international distress symbol, SOS.

Beacon relevance: Ground-to-air symbols are passive daytime signals — they require construction time and are only visible when an aircraft is overhead and looking. Beacon is the active complement: a strobe that works day or night, requires no construction, and actively catches attention rather than waiting to be noticed.
Stalled Vehicle — Desert

At night, attract attention any way you can: Set out an emergency light, turn on the car flashers or turn signals, leave the inside dome light on, or if possible build a fire outside.


Use the car mirror as a signaling device as described in the “Plane Signals” section.

Beacon relevance: “Attract attention any way you can” — this is the emergency mindset Beacon serves. The car mirror as improvised signal mirror validates that reflective signaling is universal enough to be taught in multiple BSA pamphlets. An emergency strobe (Beacon’s primary function) replaces the car flashers/dome light for a person on foot, in water, or away from a vehicle.
Safety Rule of Three

Do not travel alone; one buddy is good, but three or more hiking together is better. If one person gets hurt, the second can perform first aid and stay with the victim while the third can get help.

emergency-responders/land/bsa/bsa-first-.content:
BSA First Aid
Source: First Aid, Scouting America Merit Badge Series, 2026 printing, 108 pages. ISBN 978-0-8395-3301-8.
Hypothermia — Cold Water Risk

Hypothermia occurs when a person’s body is losing more heat than it can generate. It is a danger for anyone who is not dressed warmly enough, although exposure to cold is seldom the only cause. Dehydration is a common contributing factor to hypothermia. Wind, rain, hunger, and exhaustion can further compound the danger.


Temperatures do not need to be below freezing. A hiker caught out in a cool, windy rain shower without proper rain gear can be at great risk. A swimmer too far out in chilly water or a paddler who capsizes also is at risk for hypothermia.

Beacon relevance: BSA explicitly names “a paddler who capsizes” as a hypothermia risk — directly connecting water immersion to cold injury. This is Beacon’s primary use case: a paddler in the water, unable to self-rescue, losing body heat. The faster they’re found, the less hypothermia progresses. Beacon’s strobe accelerates detection, which reduces immersion time, which directly reduces hypothermia severity.
Hypothermia Progression

A hypothermia victim may experience numbness, fatigue, irritability, slurred speech, uncontrollable shivering, poor judgment or decision making, and loss of consciousness. Shivering that stops without rewarming is a sign that the person’s condition is worsening. He or she needs immediate medical care.

Beacon relevance: The progression from shivering → poor judgment → loss of consciousness tracks with immersion time. A victim who activates Beacon at the “uncontrollable shivering” stage gives rescuers a target before they reach “loss of consciousness” — when they can no longer assist in their own rescue.
Hypothermia Treatment

Treat a hypothermia victim by preventing the person from getting colder. Move the person into the shelter of a building or a tent. Remove wet clothing. Do not rewarm the person too quickly (for instance, by immersing the person in warm water); doing so can cause an irregular and dangerous heartbeat.

Beacon relevance: The treatment protocol emphasizes speed: prevent further cooling. Every minute a victim is in cold water deepens hypothermia. Beacon reduces search time, which reduces immersion time, which improves treatment outcomes.
Dehydration — Cold Weather Risk

Dehydration increases the danger of frostbite, so cold-weather travelers must be just as diligent about drinking fluids as they are when the weather is hot. Dehydration can play a significant role in a number of serious conditions, including heat exhaustion, heatstroke, hypothermia, and frostbite. Dehydration can happen in hot- and cold-weather conditions.

Beacon relevance: Dehydration is a compounding factor across all environmental emergencies — it accelerates hypothermia, heat exhaustion, and frostbite. A lost hiker who is dehydrated deteriorates faster. Beacon reduces time-to-find, which shortens the window during which dehydration compounds into life-threatening conditions.
Heat Exhaustion and Heatstroke

Heat exhaustion can be brought on by a combination of dehydration, exercise, and a warm environment. People with heat exhaustion usually complain that they feel really bad — lack of energy, generalized weakness, inattention, inability to concentrate, headache, nausea, faintness, and excessive sweating.


Heatstroke — much more serious than heat exhaustion — can lead to death if not treated immediately. In heatstroke, the body’s cooling system begins to fail and the person’s core temperature rises to life-threatening levels (above 105 degrees). The first sign of true heat stroke is often collapse.

Beacon relevance: Heat illness impairs judgment and mobility. A subject suffering heat exhaustion can’t self-rescue effectively — they become disoriented, weak, and unable to navigate. Heatstroke victims collapse. In both cases, the subject becomes stationary — exactly the condition where a strobe is most effective. The subject can’t walk out, but they can be found if they’re visible.
Frostbite

Frostbite is a condition that occurs when skin is exposed to temperatures cold enough that ice crystals begin to form in the tissues. Far from the warmth of the body’s core, toes and fingers are especially vulnerable, as are the nose, ears, and cheeks.

Beacon relevance: Frostbite impairs fine motor control — a subject with frostbitten fingers may not be able to operate a radio, phone, or GPS. Beacon requires one button press to activate. The single-button interface is designed for this exact scenario: compromised hands in cold conditions.
First-Aid Kit Contents
The BSA personal first-aid kit includes: gauze bandages and pads, adhesive bandages, soap, antibiotic ointment, roller bandages.
The home/patrol first-aid kit adds: adhesive tape, assorted roller bandages, triangular bandages, elastic bandages, scissors, tweezers, safety pins, non-latex disposable gloves, breathing barrier, thermometer, sterile eye dressing, instant cold pack, calamine lotion, hydrocortisone cream.
Beacon relevance: First-aid kits address injury — Beacon addresses the gap between injury and discovery. A victim who bandages their wound still needs someone to find them. The first-aid kit stabilizes; Beacon localizes. They’re complementary tools in the same emergency timeline.

emergency-responders/land/bsa/bsa-lifesa.content:
BSA Lifesaving
Source: Lifesaving, Scouting America Merit Badge Series, 2026 printing, 96 pages. ISBN 978-0-8395-3297-2.
Rescuer Safety First

Rule No. 1: Don’t become victim No. 2. Some people drown in futile attempts to save others, but those would-be rescuers are usually frantic friends or relatives whose swimming skills are little better than those of the person in distress. They lack basic lifesaving training and act inappropriately.


Do not risk injury to yourself when a rescue appears futile. Go for help rather than going into the water.

Beacon relevance: Rescuer safety is the cardinal rule — and it reinforces why signaling matters before rescue. A subject who can signal for help doesn’t need a bystander to attempt a dangerous water entry. Beacon enables the subject to summon professional rescuers rather than forcing untrained bystanders into the water.
The Rescue Hierarchy: REACH–THROW–ROW–GO

The order of preference for rescue techniques, from simple to more complex, is easy to remember: REACH–THROW–ROW–GO. The method you select will depend on the victim’s condition and distance from shore, available aids, and the condition of the water.

Beacon relevance: The REACH-THROW-ROW-GO hierarchy is the water rescue ladder. Beacon doesn’t perform any of these — it operates BEFORE them. Beacon signals “I am here” so that rescuers can REACH or THROW from shore rather than needing to ROW or GO. Every step up the ladder increases risk to the rescuer. Beacon aims to keep the rescue at the lowest rung.
Paddlesport Drowning Statistics

According to the American Canoe Association, 85 percent of canoeing victims and 48 percent of kayaking victims were not wearing life jackets. Unfortunately, they could not swim well enough at the time to save themselves.

Beacon relevance: This is direct data supporting Beacon’s primary market. Paddlesports — canoeing and kayaking — are high-risk activities where victims are often not wearing PFDs and cannot self-rescue. Beacon is designed to attach to a PFD. A paddler wearing both a PFD and Beacon converts from “victim who can’t swim to safety” to “victim who can signal for rescue while floating.”
Reaching Rescues

Reaching rescues are safe, simple, and effective. If you are out of the water and spot someone in trouble, lie down at the edge, extend a hand, and pull the victim to safety. With reaching rescues, always lie down first or otherwise brace yourself.


Provide verbal instruction and assurance to all conscious victims.

Beacon relevance: A reaching rescue requires the rescuer to SEE the victim — to spot them in the water. At night, in fog, in waves, a person’s head is nearly invisible at any distance. A strobe makes the victim visible, enabling the reaching rescue that would otherwise be impossible because the rescuer can’t find them.
Throwing Rescues

For victims beyond your reach, an aid may be thrown from shore. A floating aid with a line attached is best because it provides support and allows you to pull the victim in. Always keep track of a victim’s location. If the victim submerges, you need to know where he or she went down. Work with a partner if possible. One person can act as a spotter while the other gathers equipment and performs the rescue.


Active drowning victims cannot reach for aids.

Beacon relevance: “Always keep track of a victim’s location” — this is the visual tracking problem Beacon solves. A spotter who loses visual contact with a victim can’t direct a throw. A strobe maintains visual contact even in poor conditions. The fact that “active drowning victims cannot reach for aids” underscores that Beacon’s strobe is the right tool: it doesn’t require the victim to do anything except have it attached and activated. The strobe does the work.
Pinpointing a Victim’s Location — Visual Bearings

The farther away you are, the harder it will be to keep track of where the person went down. As you approach, line up the victim with two marks on the shore, a shorter one in front of a taller one. Two fixed points are required to define the line. The only sure way to pinpoint a location on the water is for two or more people to align objects with the victim from different locations. The spot where the lines of sight cross is where the person went down.

Beacon relevance: This bearing technique depends entirely on continuous visual contact. A victim who submerges before bearings are taken is lost. A strobe provides continuous visual reference — the spotter can maintain the bearing as they approach, and can re-acquire it if lost. At night, the strobe is the bearing point itself; no shore objects needed.
Rowing Rescues — Motorboat Safety

With motorboats, cut the engine before the final approach to avoid injuries from the prop. If only single-person kayaks are available, consider launching two kayaks for the rescue rather than one. Keep the victim in sight at all times. Shout encouragement and instructions to the victim.

Beacon relevance: “Keep the victim in sight at all times” is the constant refrain across every rescue method. The strobe makes this possible. In rough water, at distance, in low light — the strobe is what the boat crew tracks.
The Four A’s of Rescue

Recall the four A’s appropriate for any rescue: Awareness, Assessment, Action, and Aftercare.

Beacon relevance: Beacon operates at the Awareness stage — it makes rescuers aware that a victim exists and where they are. Without Awareness, the other three A’s never activate.
Noncontact vs. Contact Rescues

In noncontact rescues, the victim grasps the rescue aid you provide. Noncontact rescues are the first choice for active victims. In contact rescues, you grasp the victim and the aid. Contact rescues are normally required for unconscious victims.

Beacon relevance: A conscious victim with Beacon can be located via strobe — a noncontact rescue becomes possible because the rescuer can approach from a distance guided by light. For an unconscious victim, Beacon still marks position — enabling a contact rescue with a known target rather than an expanding search area.

Strobe vs Constant On — strobe visibility in water rescue
Visibility range — detection distance over water

emergency-responders/land/bsa/bsa-search.content:
BSA Search and Rescue Merit Badge
Source: Search and Rescue, Scouting America Merit Badge Series, 2026 printing, 100 pages. ISBN 978-0-8395-0014-8.
SAR Doctrine for Scouts
The BSA Search and Rescue pamphlet is a formal training document that teaches Scouts the structure, tactics, and personal preparedness required for SAR operations. While written for youth, it reflects real SAR doctrine — the Incident Command System, search tactics, gear requirements, and safety protocols that professional SAR teams use.
Incident Command System

The Incident Command System (ICS) is a systematic approach to the management of emergency incidents. Used by fire departments, emergency medical services, law enforcement agencies, and search and rescue teams to manage all types of emergencies, this system is flexible and scalable to all types and sizes of incidents and events.


In 2003, Homeland Security Presidential Directive 5 (HSPD-5) mandated that all federal agencies use ICS to manage all incidents.

Beacon relevance: ICS is the operational backbone of every SAR mission. Beacon operates at the tactical edge — the searcher on the ground, the subject in the water — but the framework that deploys that searcher is ICS. Understanding ICS means understanding the chain that Beacon plugs into.
SAR Organizations Named

Members of search and rescue teams are nearly all volunteers. The Air Force Rescue Coordination Center serves as the single agency responsible for coordinating land-based federal SAR activities in the 48 contiguous states. Search and rescue is one of the United States Coast Guard’s oldest missions. The National Association for Search and Rescue (NASAR) is a nonprofit organization that promotes development and improved coordination among all SAR resources. The Mountain Rescue Association (MRA), National Ski Patrol, dive teams, cave rescue groups, and four-wheel drive clubs all stand ready to assist with SAR.

Beacon relevance: The SAR ecosystem is a coalition of volunteer organizations coordinated by federal agencies. BSA explicitly names USCG, NASAR, MRA, and CAP — the same organizations the Beacon coalition targets.
Search vs. Rescue

A search is an emergency situation requiring a team of trained searchers to locate a missing person. A rescue is an emergency situation where a person’s location is known — perhaps having just been found by searchers — and he or she must be removed from danger and returned to safety.

Beacon relevance: Beacon addresses the search phase — making the subject findable. It does not perform rescue. This matches the SAR community’s understanding that finding and extracting are separate operations with separate tools.
SAR Team Ready Pack — Gear Requirements

Search team members are called out on very short notice, so having a pack ready to go, called a ready pack, is important. Most search teams require their members to have sufficient clothing and gear to enable them to stay in the field for at least 24 hours.

The BSA-specified SAR team gear list includes:
Clothing:

Sturdy hiking boots, work gloves, head cover, waterproof/windproof outer layer
“Cotton kills” — wool or synthetic only

Mandatory gear:

Food for 24 hours (high caloric)
Water (2 quarts minimum)
Swiss Army knife or multitool
Fire starter
Compass with 5° accuracy
Map of search area
Whistle
Signal mirror
Two light sources (flashlight and/or headlamp, plus extra batteries and replacement bulbs)
Personal first-aid kit
Space blanket
Pencil/pen and waterproof paper
20 feet of 1-inch tubular nylon webbing

Optional gear:

Safety equipment, generally including an ANSI Class 2 vest

Beacon relevance: The BSA’s SAR gear list is a direct source of truth for what professional searchers carry. Both a whistle and a signal mirror are mandatory — not optional, not recommended, mandatory. Two light sources are mandatory. This is the SAR community’s own doctrine saying: when you go to find someone, you bring signaling tools. Beacon combines the mirror, the strobe (one of the two light sources), and adds IR detection that no ready pack includes. A SAR team member equipped per BSA standards would immediately recognize Beacon as filling their signaling category — and adding a capability (IR strobe) they don’t carry.
Passive Search Tactics — Attracting Lookouts

A passive tactic that is often effective is an attracting lookout. A two-person team is sent to a high location to try to spot the subject’s campfire or tent, or another sign that the subject may be in the area. The team may build a campfire or use strobe lights, whistles, air horns, sirens, or other signals in the hope that the subject will respond.

Beacon relevance: This is a critical passage. BSA SAR doctrine explicitly names strobe lights as a passive search tactic — used by searchers to attract a subject’s attention, and by extension, employed by subjects to attract searchers. Beacon is exactly this: a strobe light that the subject carries, which turns passive (waiting to be found) into active (signaling to be found). The pamphlet validates the tactic from the searcher side; Beacon performs it from the subject side.
Sound Sweeps

To conduct a sound sweep, each searcher must have a radio and a whistle. They space themselves along the search area boundary at a distance of 500 to 600 feet. The team leader may transmit “whistle blast in five seconds” and then count down “5-4-3-2-1.” All searchers blow their whistles simultaneously and then listen for a response from the subject.

Beacon relevance: Sound sweeps are the audio equivalent of what Beacon does visually. Multiple searchers coordinate a simultaneous signal (whistle blast) and listen for response. Beacon does the same in the visual domain — a coordinated strobe pattern that searchers look for. The 500-600 foot spacing translates to visual search spacing: searchers spread at that distance would need to see a light source at 250-300 feet minimum — well within Beacon’s visual range.
Night Search Doctrine

Night searches are very difficult and present added hazards. Advantages: dogs work better at night, tracks show up better when illuminated by flashlights, footprints do not dry up as quickly, human voices carry farther, and subjects usually hole up rather than travel at night.


Disadvantages: searchers have an increased risk of accidents, it is more difficult to transport a subject if found, clues may be destroyed or missing, lights hamper searchers’ night vision, and it is difficult for the team leader to see and guide team members.

Beacon relevance: The night search advantages cascade directly to Beacon: subjects hole up (stationary = predictable position), voices carry farther (a subject who hears searchers can activate Beacon as response), and the strobe is visible at ranges far exceeding voice. The disadvantages — lights hampering night vision — actually favor Beacon: searchers want to see a light at night; it’s their own flashlights that degrade natural night vision. A subject’s strobe is a signal, not an impediment.
Probability of Detection (POD)

POD is usually expressed as a percentage value, i.e., “we searched our assigned area with a 50 percent POD.” In a wilderness setting, achieving a 100 percent POD is next to impossible, but the goal is to always search — and sometimes re-search — all areas of high probability until a POD of at least 85 percent to 95 percent is reached.

Beacon relevance: POD quantifies what Beacon improves. A subject without signaling capability contributes nothing to POD — searchers must visually detect a human form. A subject with a strobe increases POD because the detection cue changes from “find a person” to “find a flashing light.” The strobe is a high-contrast, active signal that pushes POD toward the 85-95% target faster than unaided visual search.
Clue Categories

Clues can be categorized into six broad areas: Physical (footprints, scent), People (witnesses), Recorded (trail register, trip plan), Event (a light, campfire, signal, human voice), Investigative, Analytical.

Beacon relevance: Under BSA SAR doctrine, a strobe flash is an Event clue — “a light, signal.” When a searcher sees a flashing light, they have an Event clue directing them to the subject. This is the doctrinal category Beacon operates in: it creates Event clues on demand.
Critical Separation

How far team members are spaced apart and still able to search effectively is called critical separation. Place an object on the ground that bears some relation to the size of the subject being sought. The team walks around the object so that it is kept in view. The distance between two opposite searchers is the critical separation.

Beacon relevance: Critical separation is determined by visual detection of a human-scale object. A strobe light effectively multiplies critical separation — searchers can detect a flashing light at far greater distance than a stationary human form. This means fewer searchers can cover the same area, or the same searchers achieve higher POD.
Becoming the Subject

Here are some pointers to keep from becoming the subject of a search and rescue: Always have a trip plan and share it with your parent or guardian. Stick to your trip plan. Know what the weather is like. Never hike or camp alone. Have proper gear and clothing.

Beacon relevance: BSA’s prevention doctrine centers on the trip plan — the same concept the SAR vault uses as the first dimension on every C Outdoor Enthusiasts page. The pamphlet reinforces that the most effective SAR tool is preparation. Beacon fits in the “proper gear” category — a signaling device that turns a prepared subject into a findable subject.
Related

BSA — organization page
Wilderness lost hiker — most common BSA-relevant scenario
Mountain Rescue Association — named as SAR partner
NASAR — named as certification body
USCG SAR Doctrine — named as SAR authority
Strobe vs Constant On — strobe tactic validated by BSA doctrine
Overt signal mirror — mandatory SAR gear
BSA Emergency Preparedness — companion pamphlet
BSA First Aid — companion pamphlet
BSA Lifesaving — companion pamphlet

emergency-responders/land/mra/mra-alaska.content:
Alaska Cold Injuries Guidelines (2014)
Source: “State of Alaska Cold Injuries Guidelines” — developed by an invited expert panel at the Southeast Region EMS Council Conference on Environmental Injuries, Sitka, Alaska. Lead authors: Ken Zafren, MD (State of Alaska Emergency Programs Medical Director) and Gordon Giesbrecht, PhD (Professor of Thermophysiology, University of Manitoba). Panel included USCG Flight Surgeon CDR Leslie Wood, MD.
Levels of Hypothermia

Accidental hypothermia is defined as the unintentional drop of core temperature to 35°C (95°F) or below.


























LevelCore TempClinical SignsMild35-32°C (95-90°F)Alert, shivering, normal/increased vitalsModerate32-28°C (90-82°F)Decreased LOC, slow heart/respiratory rate, shivering may stop below ~30°CSevere<28°C (<82°F)Markedly decreased LOC, decreased/absent response to stimuli
Beacon relevance: The transition from mild to moderate hypothermia — where shivering stops and mental status declines — is the point at which self-rescue becomes impossible. A person who can still press a button at 34°C may be unable to do so at 31°C. Beacon’s single-press activation is designed for this window: it must be activatable with the last functional motor control a hypothermic person has.
Handling Hypothermic Patients

A patient with moderate to severe hypothermia should be handled very gently and kept horizontal if at all possible. When cold, the heart is very prone to ventricular fibrillation (VF) with any disturbance. Even cautious movement of a patient may induce VF.


A severely hypothermic patient may be in a state of “suspended animation” or in a “metabolic icebox.” Severely hypothermic patients have been known to survive neurologically intact for long periods of time even if they are in asystole.

Beacon relevance: “Handle gently” — this is the medical basis for ergonomic design. A hypothermic person with cold, stiff fingers can’t manipulate small switches, complex interfaces, or multi-step sequences. The activation must be gross motor: one button, one press, unambiguous feedback.
Cold Water Drowning
Survival Time

The 1-10-1 Principle of Cold Water Immersion:

1 minute — Cold shock: gasping, hyperventilation, panic. Get breathing under control.
10 minutes — Cold incapacitation: loss of effective use of fingers, arms, and legs. Perform critical actions before this window closes.
1 hour — Hypothermia: unconsciousness from deep body cooling. Even then, survival is possible for much longer in cold water.


Beacon relevance: The 10-minute window for functional movement is Beacon’s design constraint. A paddler who capsizes in cold water has approximately 10 minutes of useful hand function. Beacon must be activatable in under 3 seconds by a person with numbing hands — before cold incapacitation closes the window. The device’s large button, one-press operation, and bar-based attachment (no threading required) are direct responses to this constraint.
Amazing Resuscitations


Lowest recorded core temperature with survival: 13.7°C (56.7°F). Patient made an excellent neurologic recovery.
Longest cardiac arrest with survival: 8 hours 40 minutes. CPR for 4 hours 49 minutes.
Longest submersion with complete neurologic recovery: 66 minutes in a 2.5 year-old child. Core temperature 19°C (66°F).



Assume that a hypothermic patient can be resuscitated even if there is an absence of vital signs or any sign of life and the pupils are fixed and dilated.

Beacon relevance: The “no one is dead until warm and dead” doctrine means SAR teams will continue searching long after conventional timelines suggest futility. A strobe that continues to flash for 26+ hours (Beacon’s battery life) aligns with the extended resuscitation window — the signal persists as long as there’s any chance of survival.
Avalanche Burial

Asphyxia is the major cause of death in avalanche burial. If the airway is patent, survival is possible for extended periods.


Only 50% of those buried survive after 30 minutes. Immediate companion rescue is critical.

Beacon relevance: An avalanche beacon (457 kHz transceiver) is the primary tool. Beacon complements it — if the victim is on the surface or partially buried, the strobe provides visual location within seconds. The IR component is detectable by helicopter FLIR even through light snow cover.
Frostbite

If frozen and rescue is near, keep frozen unless warm water thaw is available and no danger of refreezing. Refreezing after thawing causes catastrophic tissue damage.


Rewarm in warm water bath: 37-39°C (99-102°F) for 20-45 minutes or until flushing occurs.


Delay debridement or amputations for at least 21 days unless absolutely necessary. There is less tissue retraction after this time. Premature amputation may cause the loss of up to 3-5 cm.

Beacon relevance: Frostbite affects hands first — the very hands that need to activate a signaling device. The “keep frozen until definitive care” rule means a frostbitten person may be unable to self-rescue and must rely on signaling. Beacon’s large button is designed for operation with numb, stiff, or frost-nipped fingers.
Core Temperature Measurement in the Field

A rapid assessment to suggest a patient’s core temperature may be performed by placing a warm ungloved hand against the skin of a patient’s back, or chest. If the skin feels warm, hypothermia is unlikely.


Core temperature in an unconscious (intubated) hypothermic patient is best measured with an esophageal probe in the lower third of the esophagus.


Rectal and bladder temperatures are very slow to respond to changes in core temperature. During rewarming, core temperature may be rising while rectal or bladder temperature is still falling.

Helicopter Transport of Cold Patients

When moving hypothermic patients by helicopter, protect the patient from wind chill caused by rotor wash. Rotor wash can be eliminated if the helicopter shuts down while loading and unloading, but this is usually not practical in the field and is very time consuming.

Beacon relevance: Rotor wash during helicopter extraction adds wind chill to an already hypothermic patient. An attached Beacon strobe continues flashing through the rotor wash — unaffected by wind, spray, or cold — while rescuers focus on packaging the patient. It’s one less thing for the rescue team to manage.
Alaska Drowning Data (Addendum)

During 2007-2012, Alaska recorded 300 drowning deaths. Average 50 per year. 84% male. 22% occupational.


The drowning death rate among children aged 0-9 years was 2.6 per 100,000 persons, a decrease of 45% from 4.7 per 100,000 in 2000-2006.


49% of occupational boating victims were not wearing a PFD at the time of incident. 56% of non-occupational boating victims known not to have worn a PFD.


43% of adult non-occupational drowning deaths suspected or confirmed to have involved alcohol.

Beacon relevance: The PFD non-wear rate (~50%) and alcohol involvement (~43%) are the two biggest modifiable risk factors. Beacon addresses what happens after the immersion — it doesn’t prevent drowning but it dramatically improves the chance of being found by making the victim visible. This is the safety net for when the other prevention layers (PFD, sobriety) fail.
Related

Battery life targets
One Hand Activation
Waterproofing
Buoyancy

emergency-responders/land/mra/mra-avalan.content:
MRA Avalanche Facts
Source: Alpine Rescue Team — Avalanche Awareness factsheet. Fully accredited MRA member since 1962.
Survival Statistics

Someone completely buried in an avalanche has a 90% chance of survival if they’re uncovered within the first 15 minutes (absent any life threatening trauma-related injuries); someone buried 30 minutes has approximately a 50% chance of survival. The chances of survival drop dramatically after 30 minutes.

Beacon relevance: The 15-minute survival window is the companion rescue window — the victim’s party, not organized SAR, performs the recovery. An avalanche transceiver gets within 2-3 meters. A strobe confirms the exact burial position when rescuers break through the snow surface. Beacon is the final visual confirmation layer in the transceiver→probe→shovel→strobe chain.
Companion Rescue Priority

Very few people, caught in an avalanche, have been able to dig themselves out. For someone completely buried, their survival depends solely on the quick actions of the other members in their party.


“If my partner’s caught in a slide, should I immediately go for help?” NO! You are that person’s BEST chance for survival. Do everything possible to locate the subject, and then go for help.

Beacon relevance: The MRA doctrine is explicit — companion rescue first, organized rescue second. This means the signaling equipment must be usable by untrained companions, not just SAR professionals. Beacon’s single-button activation and SOS pattern are designed for this: the companion activates it, places it at the dig site, and the strobe marks the position for helicopter NVG if organized rescue arrives during the dig.
Required Equipment

Avalanche beacons, shovels and probe poles and the knowledge of how to use them are essential pieces of equipment for anyone traveling in avalanche terrain.

Beacon relevance: The standard kit is transceiver + probe + shovel. No strobe. Beacon is a fourth layer — not replacing any of the three, but adding active visual/IR signaling for the moment rescuers break through the snow surface and for helicopter detection through suspended snow dust.
Key Statistics

Most avalanches occur on slopes of 30-to-45 degrees, during or following a major snow event
Most victims have some avalanche training and consider themselves skilled
Colorado: ~6 avalanche fatalities/year, approximately 1/3 of all US avalanche deaths
Major snow event: 1”/hour for several hours, or 10” in 24 hours
15-50 mph winds can elevate danger dramatically after as little as an hour — even without new snow

Jurisdiction

The local county sheriff is responsible for and coordinates ALL backcountry emergency responses including (non ski-area) avalanches.

Related

Avalanche burial — scenario page
Cold weather performance — battery chemistry in sub-zero conditions
Mountain Rescue Association — MRA profile
Wilderness lost hiker — related backcountry SAR scenario

emergency-responders/land/mra/mra-helico.content:
MRA Helicopters in Search and Rescue
Source: “Helicopters in Search and Rescue — Basic Level” by Charley Shimanski and Dr. Charlie Mize, Mountain Rescue Association, Rev. 12 Nov 2021.
Helicopter Roles in SAR

Modern helicopters are capable of a variety of missions, to include: The search for a lost or injured person or persons; their subsequent evacuation; the insertion or extrication of rescuers; an aerial survey of topography by search and rescue leadership.


While SAR teams will often engage planes to search large open areas for prominent clues (e.g. ski tracks, tents, downed aircraft, etc.), they turn to helicopters to search with closer detail or to explore areas more difficult to examine from fixed-wing aircraft (such as cliffs, gullies, cliff bottoms, etc.).

Beacon relevance: Helicopters are the primary aerial search platform for close-detail SAR. A strobe visible from a helicopter turns a passive search subject into an active signal. The spotter looking down into cliffs and gullies is exactly who needs to see a flashing light.
The Spotter Role

When helicopters are employed during search operations, a rescuer, called a “spotter,” will fly with the pilot. The pilot must devote full attention to the helicopter; it is the spotter, therefore, who conducts the search.


Spotters should study their topographic map carefully before takeoff, and look for prominent landmarks as soon as the ship is airborne. Finding a search subject in the middle of a rocky outcrop provides little assistance to the SAR team if you have no idea where that rocky outcrop is.


The ideal spotter will have experience with helicopter flight, and will not necessarily rely on the pilot to determine which area is best to search.

Beacon relevance: The spotter is the human detection system. They’re looking for visual anomalies — color, movement, flash. A strobe (white or IR) is the strongest possible visual cue. For NVG-equipped aircraft, the IR strobe makes the subject visible before the spotter even begins searching.
Visibility Minimums (VFR)

VFR rules dictate that there must be a minimum one-half mile visibility and a 500-foot ceiling. For National Guard helicopters, the visibility for VFR is increased to 1 mile. A pilot flying under VFR must have sight of the ground at all times.


The standards for night flying are more conservative. During night flying, VFR require a minimum three-mile visibility. The rules further demand that the pilot keep a 500-foot minimum below clouds, a 1000-foot minimum above clouds, and a 2,000-foot horizontal distance from clouds.

Beacon relevance: Night VFR requires 3-mile visibility — but that’s for the pilot to see the ground, not for seeing a person. A person in dark clothing at night is invisible at any range. A strobe makes the subject visible at the full extent of VFR conditions. The strobe effectively extends the search range to the aircraft’s VFR ceiling.
Helicopter Limitations

Altitude, weather and terrain will all affect a helicopter’s performance. All aircraft have a limited working altitude (called service ceiling), which changes with air temperature and humidity.


Turbulence is the rough air that causes sudden and undesired movement in flight. Heavy turbulence can make helicopter flight difficult or even unsafe and can render the careful control required for many rescue operations challenging or impossible.


Even if a helicopter is on its way to evacuate a victim, ground personnel should still move to the scene with all equipment necessary to transport the victim. Never assume a helicopter en route will become a helicopter on scene.

Beacon relevance: Helicopters are not guaranteed. Weather, mechanical issues, or competing missions can scrub air support. Beacon is the redundancy — it works whether the helicopter arrives or not, making the subject findable by ground teams, other aircraft, drones, or shore-based observers.
In-Flight Safety and Rescuer Preparedness

Rescuers must board the helicopter fully prepared to hike out. Flying conditions can quickly deteriorate, and a flight in does not guarantee a flight out.


Rescuers should carry a portable two-way radio capable at a minimum of transmission on air-to-ground and ground-to-ground frequencies.


If your pack is small enough and can be safely secured between your feet, you may want to keep it within reach when loading the helicopter. This way, you have your survival gear and, hopefully, a hand-held radio nearby in the case of a crippling crash.

Beacon relevance: The “always prepared to hike out” doctrine reinforces that Beacon must work independently of any rescue infrastructure. If the helicopter leaves without you, Beacon still works. If you crash, Beacon still works. It’s the survival gear between your feet.
Emergency Locator Transmitter (ELT)

Once on board the helicopter, passengers should check to see that seat belts and equipment are tightly secured. In addition, they should note the location of exits, door handles, fire extinguishers, as well as the Emergency Locator Transmitter (ELT) and its manual switch before flight.

Beacon relevance: Helicopters carry ELTs for their own emergencies. The parallel is direct: Beacon is the personal ELT — manually activated, locally detectable. Where an ELT signals via satellite for aircraft crashes, Beacon signals via light for person-in-water or person-in-woods emergencies.
One-Skid and Hover Operations

One-skid landings occur when one skid or one wheel of a helicopter is in contact with the ground while the rest of the helicopter remains suspended in the air under the power of the rotor blades.


Stepping in or out of a helicopter balancing on one skid should be done very e-a-s-i-l-y to avoid radical weight change on one side of the ship.


SAR teams should try to find or make a place to land, rather than turning routinely to high-risk one-skid or hover-loads. An ordinary evacuation of a lost subject or victim with a broken leg can transform into tragedy the instant a helicopter goes down.

Related

SAR exercise integration
USCG SAR Doctrine
USCG Rescue 21 Search Patterns
IR detection surfaces
Visibility range
Strobe vs Constant On

emergency-responders/land/mra/mra-search.filePath: Emergency Responders/Land/MRA/MRA Search and Rescue for Outdoor Leaders.md
emergency-responders/land/mra/mra-search.content:
MRA Search and Rescue for Outdoor Leaders
Source: “Search and Rescue for Outdoor Leaders” by Charley Shimanski, Mountain Rescue Association, 2008. Developed for experiential education programs that may be required to respond to SAR emergencies.
Lost Person Behavior

A summary of studies on the behavior of lost persons includes:

If a lost person finds a trail, they might get on it and run like hell — convinced they’re on the way back to their last point, when in fact they might be running FROM it
Rarely will a lost person reverse their direction on a trail
Many people ignore trails and follow their own logic — traveling in a straight line. They figure they’ll come to a road or highway — not expecting the cliff or impassable river that ultimately confronts them
Some lost people will climb/hike to the top of the closest hill to get a better view, only to find that the trees atop that hill obstruct any view
The majority of lost people will travel downhill and/or downstream
Many lost people will travel at night — even without a flashlight


Beacon relevance: Lost person behavior directly contradicts the “stay put” doctrine that SAR depends on. Lost people move — at night, without a flashlight. The search area expands with every hour. A strobe on the lost person’s pack or hung from a branch provides a fixed signal even if they continue to move. It also addresses the night travel finding: a person who will travel without a flashlight is invisible to ground searchers but a strobe on their person makes them actively visible.
The Stay Put Doctrine

“Stay put” is the most important lesson you need to give your program participants. Unless there is imminent threat of life or limb, any lost person is better off if they do not move. This will keep the search area small.


“A number of lost student incidents have been unnecessarily aggravated because students did not know what to do when separated from the patrol or instructor.” — Colorado Outward Bound School’s Course Director Manual

Beacon relevance: The standard wilderness lost hiker advice — “hug a tree” — becomes advice backed by a 2+ mile strobe. If the lost person stays put AND activates a strobe, the search transitions from grid search to “fly toward the flashing light.”
Clue-Based Search

The truth is that professional searchers are looking more for clues than for victims. There are always more clues than victims, and once clues are found, the size of the search area is significantly reduced.


To make the job of searchers easier, lost parties can leave clues such as clear footprints, arrows drawn with sticks, notes attached to trees or anything they can think of to signal rescuers.

Beacon relevance: An active strobe is the ultimate clue — it doesn’t need to be found, it announces itself. Compared to footprints (degrade in rain), arrows (require the searcher to look down), and notes (require the searcher to stop and read), a strobe visible from 2+ nautical miles through canopy gaps is a clue that finds the searcher.
Search Area Expansion

Little is lost when calling for help. A search area expands exponentially with each hour a person is lost. Call for help — even if you end up not needing it.


“Urgent response to a lost person incident reduces the size of the search area” — Managing the Lost Person Incident, NASAR

Beacon relevance: The exponential search area expansion is the core argument for immediate signaling. A 26-hour strobe activated at the moment of distress keeps the effective search area at zero — the search aircraft flies toward the signal rather than expanding a grid.
Attraction Techniques

The base camp might be a good location to practice attraction techniques — build a smoky fire, make noise, honk car horns, etc.

Beacon relevance: Every attraction technique listed is either time-limited (fire burns out), range-limited (noise carries ~0.1 mile in forest), or infrastructure-dependent (car horn requires a car). A strobe at 19,000+ mcd with 360° coverage and 26-hour runtime is a continuous attraction technique that requires no ongoing effort from the victim.
Search Methodology


All search teams must be at least 2 people
Start documenting early. The person in charge of documentation should clearly mark search assignments and departure times on a map
Designate the search areas with the highest probability and send searchers there immediately
Start a “hasty search” early on — have people run the trails and streams, etc., carrying a whistle and periodically stopping to blow it 3 times
As soon as possible, a “scent article” should be collected for search dogs
Leave notes at trailheads or other exit points telling the lost party what to do if they get there


MRA Jurisdiction and Cost

The Mountain Rescue Association, representing thousands of rescue mountaineers nationwide, does not charge for its services.


In the United States, search and rescue generally falls under the jurisdiction of the county sheriff or state police.

Related

Wilderness lost hiker — primary scenario
Mountain bike crash — similar terrain, different mechanism
Hunting accident — remote area search
Mountain Rescue Association — MRA profile
NASAR — complementary SAR standards organization

emergency-responders/land/mra/mra-situat.content:
MRA Situational Awareness in Mountain Rescue
Source: “Situational Awareness in Mountain Rescue” by Charley Shimanski, Mountain Rescue Association, © 2017.
Definition

“Situational Awareness” is “the degree of accuracy by which one’s perception of his/her current environment mirrors reality.” The essence of Situational Awareness is fairly simple… perception vs. reality.


Situational Awareness is not an event, but rather a process that only ends when the SAR incident is concluded.

Beacon relevance: Situational awareness applies to the lost subject too. A person who knows they’re visible (strobe active) behaves differently than one who feels invisible. SA is a two-sided problem — the searcher’s awareness and the subject’s awareness of being findable.
The Three Stages


The perception of the relevant information
The comprehension and interpretation of that information
The projection of their states into the future


Stage I — Perception

In conventional search missions, rescuers are looking for CLUES more than they are looking for the missing subject. Why? Simply because there are far more clues than there are missing subjects, and by finding and following clues, one can find the missing subject much more quickly.


Many accidents in search and rescue operations result from a series of different things happening. There are often a number of contributing factors that, if occurring individually, might not have resulted in an accident.

Beacon relevance: A strobe is the ultimate clue — it doesn’t need to be found, it announces itself. Where searchers spend hours looking for footprints, broken branches, and other passive clues, an active strobe says “here I am” to every searcher in visual range simultaneously. This collapses Stage I (perception) to near-zero time.
Stage II — Comprehension Requires Training

The key to this stage of Situational Awareness is that it requires one to have and utilize key training and experience. For example, a rescuer in a high mountain rescue might have already perceived that the temperature is very hot. Still, without proper training in helicopter management, that rescuer might not be able to interpret that the high temperatures will have an effect on the rescue team’s use of helicopter resources.

Stage III — Projection

If you want to know if it is going to rain, you don’t look for rain, you look for CLOUDS. If you look for rain alone you’ll only know that rain is coming at the very moment that it arrives. Looking for rain alone would mean that you are only OBSERVING and INTERPRETING, but not PROJECTING into the future.


Visualize While En Route to a Call. In many emergency medical training programs, students are taught the value of visualizing the scenario prior to arriving at the rescue call.

Seven Factors That Reduce Situational Awareness (US Navy)


Insufficient or poorly communicated information and communication
Fatigue and stress
Task underload
Task overload
Group mindset
“Press on Regardless” philosophy
Degrading operating conditions


Task Underload

Task underload was a factor in a fatal plane crash during a search operation in Colorado in 1988. While flying at their assigned altitude of 13,000 feet during a search, the plane’s pilot told the spotter that he was bored, and that they were going to fly at 11,000 feet. He descended to this lower altitude. Shortly thereafter, the plane crashed in a downdraft.

Beacon relevance: Task underload during grid searching is exactly when a strobe flash catches attention. A flashing light breaks the monotony — it’s an attention anchor that counteracts the boredom factor that degrades search effectiveness.
Group Mindset and “Press on Regardless”

An accident investigation board was assembled in 1967 after the Apollo I disaster in which Gus Grissom, Edward White, and Roger Chaffee were killed in a fire aboard the spacecraft. In assembling their list of contributing factors, the Board coined a new phrase: “Go Fever.”


“The decision to delay a shuttle launch had developed into an ‘unwanted’ decision by the members of the Shuttle team.”

Overconfidence Based on Experience

Dr. Ken Kamler hypothesized from the 1996 Mt. Everest tragedy that some of the climbers were lulled into a false sense of security on the mountain, due in large part to the fact that they had successfully climbed Mt. Everest many times before. “Familiarization, and prolonged exposure without incident, leads to a loss of appreciation of risk.”


Most avalanche victims have had avalanche training. It is not the novice who is normally caught in an avalanche, but rather the experienced and educated winter backcountry traveler.

Beacon relevance: Experienced outdoor users are the most likely to need rescue — and the most likely to underestimate their risk. Beacon’s target market isn’t just beginners; it’s the experienced paddler who “doesn’t need safety gear” because they’ve never had an incident. The familiarity-risk paradox makes experienced users the highest-value segment.
Common Errors by Stage





















StageErrorI — PerceptionToo much/too little information, failure to review, too much stressII — ComprehensionToo little experience, using previous experience incorrectly, misinterpreting dataIII — ProjectionDoing things the same way over and over, not questioning what you’re doing

“You need to be always questioning what you’re doing. This is especially true in SAR operations where your expectations are not being met.”

Ten Ways to Avoid Loss of SA (US Navy)


Actively question and evaluate your mission progress
Analyze your situation
Update and revise your image of the mission
Use assertive behaviors when necessary
Make suggestions
Provide relevant information without being asked
Ask questions as necessary
Confront ambiguities
State opinion on decisions/procedures
Refuse unreasonable requests


Break the Chain

Most accidents occur when a number of elements go wrong and compound upon each other.


In a search for a missing party, rescuers suddenly asked themselves if they would change their search methodology if they presumed that the missing subject was suicidal. They made the appropriate changes, and indeed found the subject within a matter of a few hours.

Beacon relevance: The “break the chain” concept maps to the lost person’s decision chain: delay calling for help → move from last known position → night falls → searchers expand grid → hours compound. A strobe breaks the chain at any point — the moment it’s activated, the search area stops expanding.
Useful Tips


If something doesn’t look or feel right, then it probably isn’t right
Watch out when you are busy or bored
Old habits are hard to break
Expectations can reduce awareness
Things that take longer are less likely to get done right
It’s hard to detect something that isn’t there
Distraction comes in many forms
Don’t rely on reliable systems
Don’t get too excited if an emergency occurs. That’s when you’ll make poor decisions
Prevention is the best way to avoid emergencies, and Situational Awareness is one of the best forms of prevention there is

Search and Rescue Scenarios
SAR Coalition feedback loop
SAR exercise integration

emergency-responders/land/mra/mra-triage.content:
MRA SAR-Related Triage and Dispatch Guidelines
Source: “SAR-Related Triage and Dispatch Guidelines for Emergency Managers, Incident Commanders and Communications Personnel” by Tim Kovacs, Mountain Rescue Association, © 2001-2005, last updated December 11, 2009. Approved by MRA Medical Committee and Education Committee, 2004.
Core Philosophy: Proactive Dispatch

An agency can not claim to have a professional or adequate SAR system, unless it has developed a proper dispatch and response system that favors the most efficient, quick, complete and appropriate response to the victims.


“We should never sacrifice service to the citizen by delaying the dispatch of a qualified resource simply because it is a volunteer unit. Deploy the closest and most appropriate resources to the incident regardless of type of agency and regardless of paid or unpaid status.”


“To serve the public properly and to lower liability, we should look for a reason to dispatch (a SAR team), rather than look for a reason to not dispatch (a SAR team).”

Beacon relevance: The philosophy of proactive dispatch — “send now, cancel later” — directly informs why civilians need personal signaling. SAR dispatch may take hours; even a proactive agency can’t beat the clock. A strobe that the subject activates immediately eliminates the “find them” phase entirely when rescuers are within visual range.
Proactive vs. Minimalist-Reactive

While fire-EMS tends to dispatch based on sending multiple and specific resources early (proactive vs. reactive) and canceling resources only once an on-scene unit determines they are not needed, law enforcement tends to dispatch minimal and non-specialized units (reactive vs. proactive) and add more as needs are “confirmed” by on-scene officers.


Delays in dispatch of a SAR unit can easily result in up to and beyond a 2 hour delay of a qualified team into a wilderness area.


“If it mentions or hints of an injury, don’t delay, don’t wait. That is, if there is a mention or hint of an injury, dispatch SAR and EMS resources immediately. Then, cancel them later.”

Beacon relevance: A 2-hour dispatch delay is the difference between finding a subject at dusk vs. searching in darkness. A strobe shortens the active search phase from hours to minutes once the helicopter or ground team is within visual range — it partially compensates for the dispatch delay inherent in wilderness SAR.
Dispatch Priority Levels
The guide assigns four priority levels:

P1 — Immediate life threat, Code 3 (lights & siren), ALS
P2 — Urgent but not immediately life-threatening, Code 2
P3 — Non-urgent, Code 2
P4 — Routine/administrative


The goal is to send the highest possible quality search and rescue services in any situation. If in doubt, the Communications Specialist or decision-maker should treat an incident as a rescue with a savable life until confirmed otherwise.

Missing Person Protocol

Missing, Runaway or Overdue and not known to be going to a wilderness-type area: Follow standard local policy and/or consult SAR Coordinator. Never completely rule out the wilderness or SAR involvement on a missing or overdue person report.


Alzheimer’s and Depression Dementia: These must each be very aggressively approached. The subject can wander great distances, into previously unpredicted areas, and may appear normal to the untrained person.

Key questions for missing person reports:


Where did you last see the missing person?
Where exactly were they planning to go (from and to)?
When were they due back?
Did they take overnight and foul weather gear?
How many days food and water did they have?
Did they take a cell or satellite phone?
Did they have some other track-able device?
Have they ever been lost or missing before? What was the outcome?


Beacon relevance: Question 7 — “Did they have some other track-able device?” — is the Beacon question. If the answer is “they had a personal strobe,” the search transitions immediately. The dispatch protocol already asks for tracking devices; a strobe that makes the subject visually trackable fits this category perfectly.
Avalanche Protocol

Only 50% of those buried survive after 30 minutes. Undelayed dispatch is critical.


Only trained personnel should risk going into the avalanched zone. Public education is geared toward training outdoor users for avalanche safety and rescue, and the trained companions of victims are typically told to “not go for help unless it can be there 5 minutes or less” as the victims’ best chance is rescue by those already on scene.

Dispatch for avalanche:

Local Avalanche rescue team: mountain rescue and ski patrol
Avalanche dogs
Helicopter to transport hasty team of avalanche dog and rescue team
SAR Coordinator
Local EMS at the ALS level to stage in safe area out of hazard zone

Beacon relevance: The 30-minute survival window for avalanche burial is the most time-critical SAR scenario. A strobe doesn’t help under snow — but an avalanche beacon does. Beacon’s relevance is for the companion rescue scenario: the trained companions searching with beacons benefit from a visual signal on the surface if the victim is partially buried or the beacon brings them close. More broadly, the avalanche protocol demonstrates the SAR community’s recognition that some scenarios demand sub-30-minute response — intervals where civilian-carried signaling is the only viable bridge.
Helicopter Crash / Aircraft Incident Protocol

Aircraft crashes anywhere in your jurisdiction occurring farther than ¼ mile from the nearest 2wd access, or involving technical rescue circumstances, or involving more than 10 passengers: P1, Code 3, ALS.

Dispatch: Nearest Patrol unit, SO Technical/Mountain Rescue, Recon/SAR Aviation, SAR Coordinator, Nearest EMS at the ALS level, Nearest FD with aviation firefighting and fuel containment capabilities.
Communications Limitations

A structured interrogation is necessary to assure that the dispatcher always accomplishes four basic, but very essential tasks:

Identification of the proper response configuration and mode
Identification of the presence of conditions that may require pre-arrival instructions
Collection of information that will assist responders in preparing for and addressing the call
Collection of information that will assist in assuring the safety of patients, bystanders, and responders


NTSB Reference
Quoting from an NTSB investigation of a fatal Coast Guard SAR incident: “The overall effectiveness of the …search and rescue mission is largely a function of the readiness of its operations and communications centers.”
